lecture_context.lecture_title: Lecture 16: Vision and Language — Multi-Modal Foundation Models

unit.name: Chaining, visual programming, agents, and open research directions
unit.description: Explains how multiple models can be composed into systems that solve tasks no single model handles well.
unit.summary: The final segment defines chaining as combining different models to enable capabilities that a single model cannot perform alone [ts=3624s] [ts=3635s]. One example uses GPT-style descriptions to help CLIP classify categories it has not directly seen: when category names are unfamiliar, generated descriptions provide semantic anchors that CLIP can match to images [ts=3688s] [ts=3696s]. Another example counts people across boats by chaining object detector outputs and arithmetic rather than relying on a single multimodal model answer [ts=3776s] [ts=3817s]. The lecturer connects this to VisProg/visual programming, where GPT generates a Python program that calls specialized models such as object detectors or face detectors and combines their outputs to answer a question [ts=3837s] [ts=3881s]. In-context examples for program generation can be chosen statically or dynamically through retrieval, with dynamic retrieval performing better when the retrieval system is strong [ts=3891s] [ts=3930s]. Chaining can be costly because it may require API calls and sequentially running many models, motivating current research on distilling these capabilities into one model [ts=3958s] [ts=3973s]. The lecturer explicitly says this resembles an agent that decides which tools it needs and how to stitch them together [ts=3988s]. The segment closes by summarizing foundation models as models trained on one broad objective and generalized to many downstream applications, including classification, multimodal language tasks, masks, and chained systems [ts=4025s]. In Q&A, the lecturer notes that pointing can reduce hallucination but does not guarantee correctness, and that verifier models or tool-building systems are active research directions [ts=4096s] [ts=4154s].

key_points.text: Chaining combines different models to enable capabilities no single model can do alone.
GPT-generated descriptions can make unseen categories easier for CLIP to classify.
Counting across images can be solved by running object detection on each image and combining counts.
VisProg generates programs that call specialized models and combine their outputs to answer visual questions.
Dynamic retrieval of in-context program examples can improve program generation if retrieval is strong.
Chaining is costly because it may require API calls and sequential execution of many models.
A chained system can be viewed as an agent choosing which models or tools to use for a question.
Verifier systems can mitigate hallucinations by checking generated outputs before showing them to users.

kp_catalog.name: Chaining and visual programming
kp_catalog.description: Systems such as VisProg use LLM-generated programs to call specialized vision models and combine their outputs for complex visual tasks.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because chaining and visual programming synthesizes systems such as VisProg use LLM-generated programs to call specialized vision models and combine their outputs for complex visual tasks into an end-to-end method or integrated recipe.

kp_catalog.name: Chaining grounded VLM outputs into other models
kp_catalog.description: Grounded outputs such as points can become prompts or control signals for models like SAM or robotics planners.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because chaining grounded VLM outputs into other models clarifies grounded outputs such as points can become prompts or control signals for models like SAM or robotics planners, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Verification, distillation, and tool-building frontiers
kp_catalog.description: Current research tries to verify model outputs, distill chained capabilities into single models, and build new tools automatically when needed.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because verification, distillation, and tool-building frontiers extends the core thread to current research tries to verify model outputs, distill chained capabilities into single models, and build new tools automatically when needed without being required for the main pipeline.
